items.summary: The Court of Session Act 1821 establishes detailed regulations for procedures in Scotland's Court of Session and Court of Commissioners for Teinds, including handling bills of advocation/suspension, voting ties, officer substitutions, clerk qualifications/salaries from a fee fund, process management, brieves/services, extractors, auditors, and fee fund administration to streamline operations and replace fees with fixed pay.

items.title: Cinque Ports Act 1821
items.summary: 1821 Act continuing and amending prior laws to prevent frauds and thefts by boatmen, pilots, and others in the Cinque Ports jurisdiction; establishes local commissioners for quick salvage and service dispute resolution, criminalizes interference with buoys/anchors and mishandling of wrecked goods, mandates deposit of found maritime items, regulates marine stores dealers with record-keeping, permits, and advertising requirements.
items.reason: This archaic Act protects maritime property rights and provides rapid local arbitration for salvage disputes, incentivizing rescue services and deterring theft in a specific historical coastal area. However, it imposes rigid bureaucratic processes (commissioner appointments, oaths, appeals, dealer permits/books/advertisements, harsh felonies/transportation) with unseen costs: compliance burdens on small marine traders, potential for local cronyism despite oaths, and jurisdictional overlaps/conflicts. Modern institutions (Merchant Shipping Act 1995, Salvage Convention 1989, Theft Act 1968, common law contracts/insurance) handle salvage, theft, and disputes more efficiently via courts/police without distorting incentives or requiring obsolete Lord Warden oversight; deletion reduces legal clutter and deadweight costs without harming property rights or trade.
